title: Stanford Challenge
seoDescription: Take a fun Stanford-style staged reasoning quiz with 80 questions across innovation, logic, patterns, evidence, and practical decision-making.
pageTitle: Only 5% Can Pass This Stanford Challenge
summary: A fun 80-question Stanford-style challenge built around curiosity, practical reasoning, numbers, patterns, and smart decisions.

homepage.title: Can You Pass This Stanford Challenge?
homepage.summary: Can your problem-solving stay strong as each round becomes harder?

footer.aboutText: This Stanford-style challenge is a staged reasoning quiz built around clear thinking, creative problem-solving, pattern recognition, evidence reading, and practical decision-making. Stanford University opened in 1891 and is widely associated with research, innovation, and practical ideas. This quiz uses that energetic problem-solving feel for entertainment, curiosity, and self-challenge, not as an official Stanford University examination or official assessment.

landing.quickStartText: The first questions feel simple. The later ones are not.

profiles.title: You handled the hardest rounds.
profiles.copy: Excellent reasoning across the full challenge. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Strong performance.
profiles.copy: You stayed accurate as the questions became more demanding. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Good score.
profiles.copy: You completed the core reasoning rounds well. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Keep improving.
profiles.copy: The later rounds reward calm pattern recognition and careful logic. Your best area was {stage}.

stageGroups.title: Idea Warm Up
stageGroups.encouragement: Good start. The next round adds simple evidence and planning.

questions.prompt: Which answer best shows curiosity?
questions.explanation: Curiosity starts with asking why something happened.

questions.prompt: A team has 7 ideas and removes 2. How many ideas remain?
questions.explanation: 7 minus 2 equals 5.

questions.prompt: Which item does not belong?
questions.explanation: The others are tools; a question is an idea.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 1, 3, 5, 7, ?
questions.explanation: The sequence increases by 2.

questions.prompt: All project notes are saved. This document is a project note. What must be true?
questions.explanation: Every project note is saved.

questions.prompt: Which plan is most specific?
questions.explanation: It gives a specific action and step.

questions.prompt: A group builds 3 prototypes each week for 4 weeks. How many prototypes is that?
questions.explanation: 3 times 4 equals 12.

questions.prompt: A prototype fails twice. What is the most useful response?
choices: Test one change at a time
questions.explanation: Changing one thing at a time helps identify the cause.

stageGroups.title: Innovation Basics
stageGroups.encouragement: Nice momentum. Next comes more careful pattern spotting.

questions.prompt: A graph line rises slowly from left to right. What does it usually show?
questions.explanation: An upward line usually indicates growth.

questions.prompt: Which statement is evidence?
choices: The test time fell from 10 seconds to 7 seconds
questions.explanation: A measured time change is evidence.

questions.prompt: If 5 sensors each record 2 readings, how many readings are recorded?
questions.explanation: 5 times 2 equals 10.

questions.prompt: Which pair is most similar?
questions.explanation: A prototype is a kind of model.

questions.prompt: What comes next: red, green, red, green, ?
questions.explanation: The pattern alternates red and green.

questions.prompt: If every tested design has a label, and this design has no label, what follows?
choices: It was not tested by that rule
questions.explanation: A tested design must have a label.

questions.prompt: Which question best checks a bold claim?
questions.explanation: Evidence is central to checking a claim.

questions.prompt: A team has too many possible features. What should it do first?
questions.explanation: Ranking by user need supports a practical decision.

stageGroups.title: Pattern Round
stageGroups.encouragement: Good pattern work. Next comes everyday design choices.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 4, 7, 10, 13, ?
questions.explanation: The sequence adds 3.

questions.prompt: Which number is smallest?
questions.explanation: 0.7 is the smallest value.

questions.prompt: A battery is 80% full, then uses 25 percentage points. What remains?
questions.explanation: 80 minus 25 equals 55.

questions.prompt: Blueprint is to building as plan is to what?
questions.explanation: A plan guides action as a blueprint guides a building.

questions.prompt: All robots in Lab A use wheels. Unit 4 is in Lab A. What must be true?
choices: All wheeled robots are in Lab A
questions.explanation: Every Lab A robot uses wheels.

questions.prompt: Which summary fits: 'The tool is simple, but limited'?
choices: It is easy to use but has constraints
questions.explanation: Simple but limited means usable with constraints.

questions.prompt: A value changes from 15 to 45. It is multiplied by what?
questions.explanation: 15 times 3 equals 45.

questions.prompt: A solution works in a small test. What should happen before a large launch?
choices: Test it in a broader setting
questions.explanation: A broader test checks whether it still works.

stageGroups.title: Design Choices
stageGroups.encouragement: Strong decision-making. The next stage adds numbers and assumptions.

questions.prompt: Which choice best shows cause and effect?
choices: Because the code had an error, the program stopped
The screen is bright and square
The table is near a window
questions.explanation: It links the code error to the program stopping.

questions.prompt: A robot moves 6 units, then 4 units, then 5 units. How far did it move?
questions.explanation: 6 plus 4 plus 5 equals 15.

questions.prompt: Which set follows the same constant-step pattern as 5, 9, 13?
questions.explanation: Both sequences add 4 each time.

questions.prompt: Which source best supports a claim about speed?
questions.explanation: Timed trial data directly supports a speed claim.

questions.prompt: If no approved parts are cracked, and Part X is cracked, what must be true?
questions.explanation: An approved part cannot be cracked.

questions.prompt: Which instruction is clearest?
choices: Run Trial 2 with the same settings
questions.explanation: It names a specific action.

questions.prompt: A 90-minute lab is split equally into 3 blocks. How long is each block?
questions.explanation: 90 divided by 3 equals 30.

questions.prompt: A feature is popular but creates errors. What should the team weigh?
questions.explanation: The real trade-off is usefulness versus reliability.

stageGroups.title: Decision Round
stageGroups.encouragement: Halfway through. The next stage asks you to control variables.

questions.prompt: Two versions are compared, but one is tested on newer devices. What is the weakness?
questions.explanation: Different devices can affect the result.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 2, 4, 8, 16, ?
questions.explanation: Each term doubles.

questions.prompt: A sample has 6 values with an average of 5. What is the total?
questions.explanation: 6 times 5 equals 30.

questions.prompt: Which question best checks whether feedback is biased?
questions.explanation: Who was asked affects how representative feedback is.

questions.prompt: Some designs are efficient. All efficient designs save energy. What follows?
questions.explanation: The efficient designs save energy.

questions.prompt: Which wording is most cautious?
questions.explanation: May improve avoids overclaiming.

questions.prompt: A process drops from 50 seconds to 40 seconds. What is the decrease?
questions.explanation: 50 minus 40 equals 10.

questions.prompt: A team must choose between speed and accuracy. What should guide the choice?
questions.explanation: The best trade-off depends on the task goal.

stageGroups.title: Variable Lab
stageGroups.encouragement: Past the midpoint now. The next round uses applied reasoning.

questions.prompt: In an experiment, why change one variable at a time?
choices: To identify what caused the result
questions.explanation: Changing one variable helps isolate cause.

questions.prompt: Which statement is an assumption?
questions.explanation: It makes a broad value claim that needs support.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 3, 9, 27, 81, ?
questions.explanation: Each number is multiplied by 3.

questions.prompt: A jar has 4 black parts and 12 silver parts. What fraction are black?
questions.explanation: 4 out of 16 simplifies to 1/4.

questions.prompt: Which result would weaken the claim that a reminder improved attendance?
choices: Attendance rose in a similar group without reminders
questions.explanation: A similar rise without reminders suggests another cause.

questions.prompt: If all released versions pass review, and Version D is released, what must be true?
questions.explanation: Every released version passes review.

questions.prompt: Which detail matters most when comparing two batteries?
questions.explanation: Usage conditions affect battery performance.

questions.prompt: A solution fails only when demand is high. What should be tested next?
questions.explanation: The failure condition should be tested directly.

stageGroups.title: Prototype Reasoning
stageGroups.encouragement: Strong work. Next comes the abstract round.

questions.prompt: A function doubles a number and adds 1. If the input is 8, what is the output?
questions.explanation: 8 doubled is 16, plus 1 equals 17.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion follows: All pilots check instruments. Mina is a pilot.
choices: All instruments are checked by Mina
questions.explanation: All pilots check instruments, so Mina does.

questions.prompt: Which pattern alternates subtract 1, multiply by 3?
questions.explanation: 5 minus 1 is 4, times 3 is 12, minus 1 is 11.

questions.prompt: A claim is based only on people who already like the product. What is the concern?
questions.explanation: The group may not represent wider users.

questions.prompt: Which phrase best defines a prototype?
choices: An early model used for testing
questions.explanation: A prototype is an early testable model.

questions.prompt: A graph has a sudden jump after a change. What should you ask?
choices: Did the change cause it or did something else happen?
questions.explanation: A jump may have multiple possible causes.

questions.prompt: A set contains only working or paused devices. A device is not working. What must it be?
questions.explanation: Within that set, not working means paused.

questions.prompt: A usual design rule blocks a useful solution. What is a flexible first step?
questions.explanation: Understanding the rule can reveal whether it applies.

stageGroups.title: Abstract Systems
stageGroups.encouragement: Excellent. The final two rounds ask for calm, careful thinking.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 1, 4, 13, 40, ?
questions.explanation: Each term is multiplied by 3 and then 1 is added.

questions.prompt: A value rises from 30 to 42. What is the increase?
questions.explanation: 42 minus 30 equals 12.

questions.prompt: If every reliable sensor is calibrated, and this sensor is not calibrated, what follows?
choices: It is not reliable by that rule
questions.explanation: A reliable sensor must be calibrated.

questions.prompt: Which criticism fits a test with no baseline measurement?
choices: It is harder to know how much changed
questions.explanation: A baseline helps measure change.

questions.prompt: A comparison uses different success definitions for two tools. What is the problem?
questions.explanation: A fair comparison needs the same definition of success.

questions.prompt: What is the median of 2, 7, 11, 14, 30?
questions.explanation: The middle value is 11.

questions.prompt: Which relationship matches researcher to experiment?
questions.explanation: A researcher designs or runs an experiment; a chef works with a recipe.

questions.prompt: A question asks how a system behaves under stress. What is being tested?
questions.explanation: Robustness is performance under difficult conditions.

stageGroups.title: Final Challenge
stageGroups.encouragement: One final stage remains. Keep the reasoning clean.

questions.prompt: A model explains all current data but makes no testable predictions. What is the weakness?
choices: It is harder to check in the future
questions.explanation: Testable predictions make a model easier to evaluate.

questions.prompt: What comes next: 2, 3, 6, 11, 18, ?
questions.explanation: The increases are 1, 3, 5, and 7, so next is plus 9.

questions.prompt: A fair die has 6 sides. What is the chance of rolling a 4?
questions.explanation: One side out of six shows 4.

questions.prompt: Which statement is equivalent to: If a device is approved, it passed inspection?
choices: If it passed inspection, it is approved
If it is not approved, it failed inspection
If it is popular, it is approved
If it did not pass inspection, it is not approved
questions.explanation: The statement about failing inspection follows from the original rule.

questions.prompt: Which objection fits: 'No errors were reported, so no errors exist'?
choices: Errors may exist without being reported
questions.explanation: Lack of reports does not prove lack of errors.

questions.prompt: A dataset is 5, 6, 6, 7, 50. Which measure is less affected by 50?
questions.explanation: The median is less affected by an extreme value.

questions.prompt: A system has many features but users need only two. What is the best design focus?
questions.explanation: Design should prioritize the most important user tasks.

questions.prompt: Which response shows good engineering decision-making?
choices: The first version is always perfect
Testing matters only if it agrees with me
Changing a design is always failure
I will update the design if testing reveals a weakness
questions.explanation: Good decision-making adapts to evidence from testing.

questions.prompt: A product is praised by early users, but all early users are employees. What is the concern?
choices: The feedback may not represent outside users
questions.explanation: Employees may not represent independent users.

questions.prompt: A sequence follows add 4, then double: 3, 7, 14, 18, 36, ?
questions.explanation: After doubling to 36, the next step is adding 4.

questions.prompt: If 4 components cost 28 units, what do 6 components cost at the same rate?
questions.explanation: Each component costs 7 units, so six cost 42.

questions.prompt: Some prototypes are fragile. All fragile prototypes need support. What follows?
questions.explanation: The fragile prototypes need support.

questions.prompt: Which assumption supports: 'Users clicked it often, so it was useful'?
questions.explanation: The argument assumes clicks reflect value.

questions.prompt: A result appears only once and cannot be repeated. What should you do?
questions.explanation: Non-repeatable results need caution.

questions.prompt: Two designs have equal average speed but different failure rates. What should be compared next?
questions.explanation: Failure rate is a reliability issue.

questions.prompt: When a constraint creates a better design, what has happened?
choices: The first idea had to win
questions.explanation: Useful constraints can focus creativity.
